artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick update before the snow hits

Hi Rach,

Sneaking this in between clients while I inhale my sad desk salad. How are things in Albany? Did you survive flu clinic season?

We’re bracing for another storm here, so I’m trying to front-load everything. Ethan’s still doing okay at Montpelier High overall. He loves his life skills class and that laminated calendar board, but I’m still on edge after the February 2024 mess at Spaulding last year when he hit his para, Jason Lee, and Barre City Police got called. Even though we avoided charges and did those three emergency IEP meetings with Kelly Donovan to overhaul his behavior plan, it sits in the back of my mind every time the phone rings during the school day.

On my end, work is…work. I still get a pit in my stomach thinking about that Vermont Office of Professional Regulation investigation back in March 2023 after I called DCF on that neglect case. Even though OPR closed it with no findings and Karen O’Rourke just had me do the 6‑week ethics refresher at UVM Medical Center, I notice my anxiety spike every time I’m about to file a report. Dr. Alan Pierce keeps reminding me that “appropriate mandated reporting is not overstepping,” but it still rattles me.

Money is tight but stable. I’ve been paying the Vermont Department of Taxes $95/month since May 2023 on that $3,200 from when I cut my hours in 2020–21. Between that and the uncovered stuff at Green Mountain ABA, I’m getting very cozy with my Excel budget.

Anyway, enough doom. Send pics of the kids’ Halloween costumes when you get a minute. Ethan still wants to be “a calendar” again this year.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Quick update before the snow hits

artifacts.content:
Title: Therapy / med check prep notes

For session with Dr. Pierce – Friday 4:30 p.m.

1. Mood / meds
- Still on sertraline 50 mg since the October 2021 Central Vermont Medical Center overnight. Overall pretty stable, but caregiving stress is creeping up again.
- Remember: that CVMC night happened after I told Dr. Lisa Hart I was having passive suicidal thoughts about all the IEP/ISP crises. Left with a safety plan and started the 50 mg. Need to talk about whether to adjust dose now that work is busier.
- Also still thinking about the longer history: major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety really ramped up after Ethan’s autism + intellectual disability diagnosis at Vermont Children’s Hospital (March 2012). By Fall 2015 I was having panic attacks in the Vermont Community Health Partners parking lot, which is when I started weekly therapy with Dr. Karen Phillips at Howard Center and went up to 100 mg sertraline back then.

2. Triggers
- Fire alarms / loud hallways still spike anxiety, probably tied to Ethan’s October 2019 elopement during the fire drill at Spaulding High in Barre and the three police calls to VCHP that month.
- Also want to mention the June 2023 Hannaford incident in South Burlington when Ethan started head-banging in the cereal aisle and I froze so badly Tom (manager) had to call Mom (Susan) to drive us home.

3. Goals
- Prevent another CVMC-level burnout.
- Talk about balancing work as a care coordinator, the tax payment plan, and Ethan’s services without sliding back into that dark place.

Subject: Need to tweak Ethan’s schedule (custody / visits)

Hi [School Social Worker Name],

I wanted to loop you in on a few things that affect Ethan’s after-school schedule and transportation.

Because of Mark’s DUI and reckless endangerment charges from July 2022 in Barre, the Washington County Family Court modified our custody order so that I have full physical custody and Mark only has supervised visits at the Barre Probation & Parole office twice a month. Practically, that means Ethan is with me almost all the time, and the P&P visits are tightly scheduled around Mark’s probation.

On the days he has those supervised visits, Ethan’s routine gets more complicated. I usually pick him up from Montpelier High, drive him to Barre P&P, and then back to our apartment on State Street afterward. He’s exhausted by the end of those evenings, and any unexpected changes (traffic, waiting room delays) really ramp up his anxiety.

This is layered on top of the February 2024 incident at Spaulding High when he hit his paraeducator, Jason Lee, and Barre City Police were called. Even though he’s at Montpelier High now and we avoided formal charges back then, the 10-day suspension and the three emergency IEP meetings we had with his Designated Agency case manager, Kelly Donovan, are still very much in his memory.

I’m hoping we can look at minimizing transitions on his visit days – maybe keeping his community support worker involved earlier in the afternoon so he’s regulated before we drive to Barre.

Happy to talk more at our next team meeting.

Best,
Laura

Laura M. Kensington, LCSW
Care Coordinator, Vermont Community Health Partners

metadata.subject: Need to tweak Ethan’s schedule (custody / visits)

artifacts.content:
Title: Ethan – Emergency IEP meeting (2 of 3)

Date/Time: Wednesday, 2024-02-21, 1:00–3:00 p.m.
Location: Spaulding High School – Special Ed conference room, Barre, VT
Attendees: Laura Kensington, Ethan’s special ed case manager, principal, school psychologist, paraeducator Jason Lee, Designated Agency case manager Kelly Donovan, behavior specialist.

Notes:
- Second of three emergency IEP meetings following Ethan’s February 2024 behavioral incident (hit paraeducator Jason Lee hard enough that Barre City Police were called). Need to finalize revisions to his behavior intervention plan and safety plan.
- Bring: copy of his current ISP from the Designated Agency, notes from incident debrief, data from community support worker about triggers and successful de‑escalation strategies.
- Emphasize: Ethan’s sensory processing challenges, anxiety around loud/crowded spaces, and need for a predictable visual schedule. Reference past elopement during fire drill at Spaulding in October 2019 and how alarms remain a trauma trigger for both him and me.
- Ask about: access to a quiet room, staff training on using his speech-generating device and picture exchange, how suspensions will be handled going forward (10‑day out-of-school suspension this time felt excessive).
- Self-care: plan something grounding afterward (short walk along the Winooski or 20‑min yoga at home) so this doesn’t spiral into another October 2021‑style burnout.

Subject: Money stuff + garden update

Hi Dad,

Thanks again for talking me down about the car repair. I ran the numbers (again) and I think I can make it work without dipping into the tiny emergency fund.

I’m still on the payment plan with the Vermont Department of Taxes for the back state taxes from 2020–2021, when I cut my hours at Vermont Community Health Partners to take care of Ethan and totally miscalculated my withholding. It was about $3,200 total, and I’ve been paying $95/month since May 2023. Between that and what insurance won’t cover at Green Mountain ABA, there isn’t a lot of wiggle room, but it’s at least predictable.

Emotionally, I’m in a better place than I was a few years ago. You remember how bad things got in October 2021, when Dr. Hart sent me to Central Vermont Medical Center for that overnight evaluation after I told her I was having passive suicidal thoughts about juggling Ethan’s IEP and ISP crises. Getting discharged with a safety plan and starting sertraline 50 mg was scary, but honestly life-saving.

I still have my ups and downs – especially when something at work pokes at old wounds, like the Office of Professional Regulation investigation in March 2023 after that DCF report – but the meds, therapy with Dr. Pierce, and our little routines (my garden, walks by the Winooski) help a lot.

On the lighter side: the community garden plot actually produced edible tomatoes this year, and Ethan loved lining them up by size. He calls them “red circles” on his device.

Love you,
Laura

Title: Supervision reflection – ethics / licensing / ADHD?

Date: 2023-11-06

Things to bring up with Karen in supervision:

1. OPR investigation fallout
- Even though the Office of Professional Regulation closed the March 2023 complaint with no findings, I still feel a wave of panic every time I consider calling DCF. The 6‑week ethics refresher at UVM Medical Center helped intellectually, but my body still reacts like I’m in trouble.

2. ADHD / executive functioning
- I keep thinking about how much I struggle with deadlines and paperwork (license renewals, CEUs, Ethan’s forms, etc.). It reminds me of that time I almost missed my LCSW recertification deadline and had to scramble for extra ethics CEUs and fees. Wondering if I should get a formal ADHD evaluation like the one Dr. Elena Hart does at the Vermont Center for Cognitive Health in Burlington.

3. Burnout history
- Important context: my depression/anxiety history goes way back – escalated after Ethan’s diagnosis at Vermont Children’s Hospital in March 2012, panic attacks in the Vermont Community Health Partners parking lot by Fall 2015, then the October 2021 overnight at Central Vermont Medical Center when Dr. Hart sent me in for passive suicidal thoughts.
- Also the high-conflict divorce period 2014–2016, the temporary relief‑from‑abuse order at Chittenden County Family Court, and that March 2015 night in the Winooski apartment when Mark pounded on the door for over an hour while Ethan was terrified. My therapist at UVM Med Center’s outpatient psychiatry clinic called that the “origin story” of the PTSD.

Goal: figure out sustainable boundaries so I can keep doing this work without repeating old patterns.

Subject: Checking in after Hannaford + ideas for next group

Hi Megan,

I’ve been thinking about you since group last week and wanted to check in. That story you shared about freezing in the Walmart parking lot after your son bolted hit close to home.

I had a similar episode in June 2023 at the Hannaford in South Burlington. Ethan started head-banging in the cereal aisle, and I just…shut down. Total dissociation. The manager, Tom, ended up calling my mom, Susan, because I couldn’t safely drive Ethan home. It scared all of us, but it also finally convinced me that my own anxiety/dissociation is part of the picture, not just his behavior.

Between that and the February 2024 incident at Spaulding High when he hit his para, Jason Lee, and Barre City Police got called (we avoided charges, but he still got a 10-day suspension and we had three emergency IEP meetings with Kelly Donovan to redo his behavior plan), I’ve been really leaning into my own supports: sertraline since the October 2021 CVMC scare, weekly-ish sessions with Dr. Pierce here in Montpelier, yoga after Ethan’s in bed.

For our next parent group at my place, I’m thinking we could focus on “meltdown aftermath” – not just for the kids, but for us. Maybe a loose agenda: practical debriefing (what worked, what didn’t), dealing with schools/police (like after our Spaulding episodes), and then 20 minutes on caregiver nervous systems – grounding, safety plans, when to call our own providers.

Also, totally mundane: can you do Thursday instead of Monday? Mondays are now tied up with Ethan’s supervised visits with Mark at the Barre Probation & Parole office, thanks to the modified custody order after Mark’s July 2022 DUI/reckless endangerment mess.

Take care,
Laura

metadata.subject: Checking in after Hannaford + ideas for next group

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Kensington <l.kensington@vchp.org>
To: Dr. Karen O’Leary <koleary@howardcenter.org>
Date: 2025-02-11 07:42 AM
Subject: Prep for Thursday session

Hi Karen,

I wanted to jot a few things down before Thursday so we can use the time well. I’ve been thinking a lot about how layered my disability picture has become over the years.

The first big crack was back in Spring 2009 during the University of Vermont MSW program – when the bipolar II really showed itself. I still remember going four nights without sleep to finish that 40-page paper for Professor Daniels, dropping it in his mailbox at 3 a.m., and then crashing so hard I missed two weeks of my Howard Center field placement. That’s when I finally filed disability accommodation paperwork with the UVM ACCESS office. It feels like the starting point of this whole journey.

Things escalated again after Ethan’s autism and intellectual disability diagnosis at Vermont Children’s Hospital in Burlington in March 2014. The chronic major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety since then have been brutal. Since late 2015 I’ve been on and off FMLA from Vermont Community Health Partners, in weekly therapy with you at Howard Center, and taking 100 mg sertraline plus 0.5 mg clonazepam at night for panic and insomnia. Lately the panic spikes again when I think about his transition to adulthood.

Could we also revisit whether the meds are still the right combo, especially with the autoimmune fatigue flares and ADHD stuff layered on top now?

Thanks,
Laura

From: Laura Kensington <l.kensington@vchp.org>
To: Megan Porter <m.porter@vchp.org>
Date: 2025-01-06 09:18 AM
Subject: ADA/FMLA + schedule adjustments for 2025

Hi Megan,

I wanted to formally document the disability-related accommodations we’ve been juggling so they’re clear in my 2025 work plan.

Physically, the chronic low-back pain from my 2011 car accident on I-89 near the Williston exit is still a major factor. I really can’t sit more than about 45–60 minutes without needing to stand or stretch, which is why I’ve had the standing desk at Vermont Community Health Partners since 2018. I also see Dr. Lisa Morrell at the UVM Spine Program every 3–4 months for injections and pain management, so I’ll keep blocking those follow-ups on my calendar.

On top of that, the autoimmune-related fatigue and pain – suspected lupus – has been flaring on and off since Winter 2021. Per Dr. Singh at the UVM Medical Center rheumatology clinic, I reduced my caseload by about 30%, and that still seems necessary. On bad days I can’t safely drive Ethan from our apartment in Montpelier to his ABA sessions at the Central Vermont Autism Center in Berlin, so my ex-husband Mark sometimes misses construction shifts to cover.

Cognitively, the ADHD combined type that Dr. Elena Hart at the Vermont Center for Cognitive Health in Burlington formally diagnosed in June 2022 continues to impact my executive functioning. It’s what led me to blow past the April 2023 LCSW recertification deadline, pay a $275 late fee to the Vermont Office of Professional Regulation, and scramble to complete an extra 6 hours of ethics CEUs.

I’m happy to meet to translate this into concrete schedule and meeting expectations.

Best,
Laura

From: Laura Kensington <laura.kensington@example.com>
To: Rachel Miller <rachel.miller@albanymed.org>
Date: 2024-09-03 10:27 PM
Subject: Catch-up (and why I’ve been weird about food)

Hey Rach,

I know you’ve noticed I’ve been cagey about food and weight again, so I wanted to be honest. You remember how rough Spring 2020 was when COVID hit and Ethan’s school-based services in Montpelier just stopped overnight? No para support, no OT, no speech – it was all on me while I was trying to keep up with telehealth for the clinic.

That loss of structure plus the stress triggered a full relapse of the eating disorder. By my August 2020 primary care visit at Vermont Community Health Partners, I’d lost 18 pounds and my BMI had dropped below 18. My PCP, Dr. Lewis, put me on weekly weigh-ins and coordinated with a dietitian and a therapist at the Women’s Eating Disorders Program at Brattleboro Retreat. It was humiliating and stabilizing at the same time.

Layer that on top of the chronic depression and anxiety that ramped up after Ethan’s March 2014 diagnosis at Vermont Children’s Hospital in Burlington, the bipolar II history from the UVM days, the suspected lupus flares since Winter 2021, the back injury from the 2011 I‑89 crash, and now the ADHD diagnosis from June 2022 – it feels like my whole body/brain is one long chart note.

I’m mostly stable now, but I still need you to nudge me if you see old patterns creeping back.

Love you,
Laura

metadata.subject: Catch-up (and why I’ve been weird about food)

artifacts.content:
From: laura.kensington@vchp.org
To: dr.melissa.chen@centralvtcounseling.org
Date: March 4, 2026
Subject: Context before Thursday’s session

Hi Melissa,

Before we meet on Thursday, I wanted to give you a bit more written context so we can use our time efficiently.

I describe my disability as a trauma-related anxiety disorder that started after Ethan’s behavioral crisis in October 2019, when he eloped from Spaulding High School in Barre during a fire drill. That month, the police called me at work at Vermont Community Health Partners three times, asking me to leave clinic to help de-escalate. I held it together in the moment, but ever since then, loud alarms, crowded hallways, or sudden yelling can trigger flashbacks and dissociation for me.

You should also know that I’ve had two brief inpatient stays at Brattleboro Retreat for inpatient psychiatric stabilization since then. They were helpful in terms of getting sleep and resetting my meds, but I still find myself bracing whenever I hear the building alarm tested at the Main Street clinic or when Ethan’s school emails about safety drills.

This is starting to interfere more with my work as a care coordinator and LCSW—especially when we have chaotic team huddles or when a client raises their voice unexpectedly—and with showing up fully for Ethan. I’d like to focus this week on building a concrete plan for managing these triggers at work and at Montpelier High, and maybe talk through whether I should formally request accommodations from HR.

Thanks for reading all this ahead of time.

Laura
